data.title: Macy's Is Maintained at Equal-Weight by Morgan Stanley
data.description: (13:48 GMT) Macy's Price Target Raised to $21.00/Share From $20.00 by Morgan Stanley

data.title: Google AI Chips Could Be a $900 Billion Goldmine -- Should You Buy GOOGL Stock Now?
data.description:
Alphabet has seen investor optimism as its in-house AI chips, called TPUs, are being valued by a top analyst at about $900 billion, after the stock jumped about 30% in the fourth quarter.

D.A. Davidson's Gil Luria, a renowned analyst, told clients that TPUs, specialized processors for machine learning used in Google Cloud, could capture roughly 20% of the AI chip market if Alphabet sells them to third parties.

Recent deals and reports have fueled the view. Alphabet said it would supply chips to Anthropic, and The Information reported Meta Platforms may pay for TPU access. That interest suggests TPUs might move beyond internal use.

TPUs are designed for specific AI workloads, unlike Nvidia general-purpose accelerators, which analysts say makes them more efficient for certain tasks.

Morgan Stanley analyst Brian Nowak estimated selling 500,000 chips to data centers could add about $13 billion to 2027 revenue and roughly $0.37 to earnings per share, underscoring potential upside while questions about go-to-market strategy remain.

Is GOOGL Stock a Buy?

Based on the one year price targets offered by 58 analysts, the average target price for Alphabet Inc is $309.96 with a high estimate of $390.00 and a low estimate of $185.00. The average target implies a downside of -2.41% from the current price of $317.62.

Based on GuruFocus estimates, the estimated GF Value for Alphabet Inc in one year is $228.14, suggesting a downside of -28.17% from the current price of $317.62. gf value is gurufocus' estimate of the fair value that the stock should be traded at. it is calculated based on the historical multiples the stock has traded at previously, as well as past business growth and the future estimates of the business' performance. for deeper insights, visit the forecast page.

data.title: Fitch Affirms Allianz SE's Insurer Financial Strength Rating at 'AA'/Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Paris-05 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Allianz SE's Insurer Financial Strength (IFS) Rating at 'AA' (Very Strong) and Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'AA-'. Fitch has also affirmed the company's main subsidiaries' IFS Ratings at 'AA'. The Outlooks are Stable. A full list of rating actions is below.

The affirmation reflects Allianz's very strong company profile, capitalisation and leverage. It also reflects the group's very strong financial performance, supported by steady and diversified earnings streams.

Key Rating Drivers

Very Strong Company Profile: Allianz's very Strong company profile reflects a leading franchise in core markets, large operating scale and extensive diversification by geography and business lines. Allianz is the largest insurance company in Germany by market share and is among the leading insurers in Europe, with a strong presence in major markets worldwide. It is also one of the world's largest asset managers, through Pimco and Allianz Global Investors.

Very Strong Capitalisation and Leverage: Allianz's Solvency II (S2) was very strong and unchanged at 209% at end-3Q25 (target: over 180%) with capital generation more than offsetting dividends and share buybacks. Its Prism Global model score was unchanged at 'Very Strong' at end-2024, although it fell slightly to near the 'Strong' category. We estimate its financial leverage ratio (FLR) to be about 19% at end-9M25, broadly unchanged from end-2024 and 2023.

We expect Allianz to maintain its capital strength with broadly constant financial leverage over the medium term, supported by sustained operating capital generation, assuming a normal level of major weather-related losses.

Diversified, Very Strong Earnings: Allianz's record of very strong profitability is supported by diversified earning sources providing stability through market cycles. The Fitch-calculated net income return on equity (ROE) rose to 16.7% in 2024 (2023: 15.1%), one of the strongest in Europe's insurance sector.

In 9M25, its operating profit rose 10.4% year on year to EUR13.1 billion, driven by all business lines, notably Property &Casualty. The combined ratio improved to 91.6% (9M24: 93%, 2024: 93.4%), driven by sustained price rises, smaller natural catastrophe losses and an improving expense ratio. We expect operating performance to further improve in 2025 and stay very strong in 2026, supporting our expectations for an ROE of 16% and above in 2025 and 2026.

Moderate Investment Risk: The insurer's investment risk is moderate, although fairly high for the rating and compared with peers'. This is reflected in a Fitch-calculated risky-assets/capital ratio of 94% at end-2024 (end-2022: 93%), mainly driven by large equity and unrated, alternative credit exposures.

Very Strong Reserves Adequacy: Allianz's reserving practices are prudent and supportive of its ratings. This is reflected in favourable prior-year development of reserves over the past five years. In 2024, these represented 2.1% of P&C revenue (2023: 3.2%).

Very Strong Asset-Liability Management: Allianz's balance sheet is largely shielded from interest rate movements, as the group's duration gap between its assets and liabilities is close to zero. Cash flow and liquid assets are sufficient to maintain strong liquidity, as surrender risk remains manageable at group level.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

- A deterioration of Allianz's capital position, with the Solvency 2 ratio falling below 180% for a prolonged period

- A sustained decline in profitability, with net ROE below 10%

- FLR above 28%

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

- A sustained, strengthening of capitalisation, leverage and financial performance metrics above those of 'AA' rated peers, manifested in the following:

- Solvency 2 ratio above 230%

- FLR below 10%

- ROE above 15%

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: Russia's national wealth fund liquid assets rise to $52.6 bln as of December 1
data.description:
Liquid assets in Russia's national wealth fund (NWF) amounted to $52.6 billion or 1.9% of projected gross domestic product as of December 1, up from $51.6 billion in the previous month, the Finance Ministry said on Friday.

Total assets in the wealth fund, which include investments in domestic infrastructure projects, rose to the equivalent of $169.5 billion or 6.1% of GDP as of December 1 from $163.99 billion as of November 1, the ministry said.

data.title: Treasury Yields Slightly Higher to Start Friday — Market Talk
data.description: It is a frigid Friday morning on the east coast and likely a cold session in the Treasurys market, with little on the agenda to drive major swings. This morning's September PCE inflation number figures to come in around 0.2% month over month, with surprises unlikely due to the calculation's basis in already-published data. "The prospects for a slow December Friday appear to be elevated," Ian Lyngen of BMO writes. "It's like being out of the office while remaining at your desk." The benchmark 10-year yield is trading around 4.114%, versus 4.107% to end Thursday. The 2-year yield is around 3.542%, versus 3.53% Thursday. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.title: Baltic index edges down on lower rates, but posts weekly rise on earlier highs
data.description:
The Baltic Exchange's dry bulk sea freight index, which tracks rates for vessels transporting dry bulk commodities, was dragged lower by weaker rates across segments on Friday, but logged a weekly gain, buoyed by near two-year highs hit earlier.

* The main index , which factors in rates for capesize, panamax and supramax shipping vessels, fell by 87 points, or 3.1%, to 2,727 points.

* The index logged its highest level since December 2023 on Wednesday, which helped it post a 6.5% weekly rise.

* The capesize index (.BACI) lost 236 points, or about 4.4%, to 5,083 points, logging a weekly gain of 13.4%.

* "Dry bulk rates have been the story in recent days with Cape rates especially, breaking new ground and pushing above $40,000/day for the first time in two years. They have eased slightly from their highs mid-week but remain elevated," analysts at Jefferies said in a note.

* Average daily earnings for capesize vessels (.BATCA), which typically transport 150,000-ton cargoes such as iron ore and coal, decreased by $1,957 to $42,151.

* Dalian iron ore futures slid on Friday and ended the week lower, as rising year-end seaborne shipments and tepid Chinese demand weighed.

* The panamax index (.BPNI) fell by 26 points, or 1.4%, to 1,837 points.

* Average daily earnings for panamax vessels (.BPWT), which usually carry 60,000-70,000 tons of coal or grain, decreased by $237 to $16,530. It was the index's seventh straight session of losses, which registered a 5.9% weekly fall.

* Among smaller vessels, the supramax index (.BSIS) lost 5 points to 1,436 points.

data.title: Jungheinrich narrows forecast after delay in sale of Russian unit
data.description:
German forklift maker Jungheinrich narrowed its full-year forecast for incoming orders on Friday due to a delay in the sale of its Russian subsidiary.

The company now expects to complete the sale of its Russian subsidiary in 2026, instead of this year, due to the Russian government commission not yet granting approval for the transaction.

Jungheinrich therefore specified that it expects incoming orders for 2025 to between 5.4 billion euros and 5.6 billion euros ($6.29 billion-$6.52 billion), versus previous guidance for between 5.3 billion and 5.9 billion.

($1 = 0.8583 euros)

data.title: Seagate Technology Initiated at Buy by China Renaissance
Netflix to Buy Warner Bros. for $83 Billion After Split. How the Stocks Are Reacting. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

Netflix has agreed to buy Warner Bros. Discovery in a $72 billion deal, following the planned spinoff of Warner's global cable network operations.

The deal values Warner Bros. Discovery at $27.75 a share, implying a total equity value of approximately $72 billion and an enterprise value of around $82.7 billion.

Netflix shares were down 1.3% in premarket trading, while Warner shares were rising 1.6%.

The deal has angered Paramount Skydance, which has accused Warner of favoring its rival bidder.

Paramount wrote in a Wednesday letter to Warner Discovery Chief Executive David Zaslav that the company has "embarked on a myopic process with a predetermined outcome that favors a single bidder," which it believes to be Netflix, the WSJ reported.

Warner, Netflix and Paramount didn't immediately respond to requests for comments from Barron's early on Friday.

Warner is currently moving ahead with plans to separate into two companies, one comprising the studio and streaming assets and the other containing its global cable network operations. Paramount sought to buy the whole company, while Comcast and Netflix have presented offers for the streaming and studios segment, not the cable channels.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Credit Could Provide More Stability in Portfolios Than Equity in 2026 — Market Talk
data.description: Building more credit-focused and less equity-focused investments is likely to provide resilience in the face of elevated volatility in 2026, Van Lanschot Kempen managing director Patrick Race says at the Edelman Smithfield Investor Summit in London. Markets are likely to stay volatile in the coming year due to geopolitical and economic uncertainty and the appointment of a new U.S. Federal Reserve chair. "I can't see things getting less volatile anytime soon," he says. Investment-grade credit, nonetheless, looks less appealing due to tight spreads. "We are diversifying away from investment-grade credit, [it] just doesn't seem to be good enough value for money," he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Biotech co Protara slides on $75 mln share sale
data.description:
** Protara Therapeutics' shares down 16.7% premarket to $5.72 after $75 mln follow-on priced

** New York City-based biotech late Thurs said it sold ~13 mln shares at $5.75, a 16.3% discount to last sale

** It intends to use net offering proceeds to fund clinical development of its cell therapy, TARA-002, and development of other clinical programs

** Co is developing TARA-002 in non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) and in lymphatic malformations (LMs), rare, non-malignant cysts of the lymphatic vascular system that primarily form in the head and neck region of children

** With ~38.6 mln shares outstanding, TARA has ~$265 mln market cap

** JP Morgan, TD Cowen and Piper Sandler are joint bookrunners for offering

** Through Thurs close, TARA shares up 30% YTD including ~58% surge this quarter

** All 6 analysts covering the stock are bullish; median PT is $25, per LSEG data
